research_series.series_name: Gut-Brain Axis, GI Symptoms, Microbiome, Fatigue, Mood, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Gut microbiota composition/function is associated with mood, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms across multiple populations

mediator.name: microbial_metabolite_immune_endocrine_vagal_pathways
mediator.pathway: microbiome to CNS via SCFA, immune, endocrine, vagal, barrier integrity pathways
mediator.mechanism: altered SCFA profiles, inflammation/endotoxemia, tryptophan-kynurenine shifts, serotonin/gut hormone signaling, vagal signaling

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Altered gut microbiome composition/function is associated with higher probability of mood, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms, but causality is usually not proven.

hypotheses.reason: Human cohort/intervention evidence supports associations, while full temporal causal chains are uncommon.

evidence.summary: Strong association evidence across populations; causal evidence remains limited by confounding and incomplete mediator-outcome chains.

key_sources.title: Gut-Brain Axis, Microbiome, GI Symptoms, and Neurobehavioral Outcomes: Human Evidence Review
key_sources.relevant_finding: Gut microbiota composition is associated with mood/fatigue/cognitive symptoms; causality remains difficult to establish.
key_sources.limitations: Methodological heterogeneity; confounding; limited full-chain longitudinal/interventional evidence.

patterns.pattern_name: IBS/IBD symptom burden is linked to fatigue, anxiety/depression, and extraintestinal symptoms

mediator.name: GI_inflammation_visceral_pain_and_GBA_dysregulation
mediator.pathway: GI symptom burden and inflammation interact with mood/fatigue via immune, vagal, and interoceptive pathways
mediator.mechanism: visceral pain, inflammation, gut-brain signaling, sleep disruption

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Higher IBS/IBD symptom burden increases likelihood of fatigue and mood symptoms, but mechanisms vary by disease activity and individual context.

hypotheses.reason: IBS/IBD populations show consistent comorbidity between GI burden and fatigue/mood symptoms.

evidence.summary: IBS/IBD are repeatedly linked to fatigue and anxiety/depression; microbiome-targeted therapies show variable/modest benefits.

limitations: Symptom burden and psychological distress are bidirectional

patterns.pattern_name: Microbiome-targeted interventions may improve subjective GI/mood/fatigue symptoms in select groups but objective cognition effects are inconsistent

mediator.name: microbiome_or_metabolite_shift
mediator.pathway: intervention alters microbiome composition/metabolites and may alter symptoms
mediator.mechanism: SCFA production, reduced inflammation, altered neurotransmitter/gut hormone pathways

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Microbiome-targeted interventions can improve subjective symptoms in select groups, but effects are modest, strain/population-specific, and not consistently seen for objective cognition or sleep.

hypotheses.reason: RCTs/meta-analyses show small-to-moderate benefits, but heterogeneity is high.

evidence.summary: Probiotic/prebiotic/FMT interventions show modest and inconsistent benefits; objective cognition/sleep effects are weak.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Full causal chain from GI/microbiome marker to mediator to neurobehavioral outcome is rarely demonstrated.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Most studies are cross-sectional or measure only two links in the chain.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Probiotics do not consistently improve objective cognition or sleep quality.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Some RCTs show subjective improvement without objective cognitive/sleep benefit.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Severity of functional bowel symptoms such as abdominal pain, bloating, constipation, and diarrhea.
Inflammatory bowel disease activity reflected by clinical activity and inflammatory biomarkers such as fecal calprotectin.
Altered gut microbiome composition/function measured by stool microbiome or metabolomic assays.
Altered levels of microbial short-chain fatty acids such as butyrate, propionate, acetate.
Shift in tryptophan metabolism toward kynurenine pathway activity.

research_gaps: Few full-chain longitudinal/interventional studies measuring GI/microbiome marker, mediator, and neurobehavioral outcome.
Objective cognition endpoints are underrepresented compared with subjective mood/fatigue.
Strain-specific probiotic effects require better standardization.
Baseline diet, medications, antibiotics, stress, and comorbidities confound many findings.

most_supported_patterns: Gut microbiome/GI symptom burden is associated with subjective mood/fatigue/cognitive symptoms across multiple populations.
IBS/IBD symptom burden is linked to fatigue and anxiety/depression.
Microbiome-targeted interventions may improve subjective symptoms in selected groups.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: GI symptom burden as a more reliable practical node than generic dysbiosis.
IBD inflammation and fecal calprotectin as stronger biological nodes when measured.
SCFA and tryptophan-kynurenine pathways as mechanistic hypothesis nodes.
patterns_requiring_caution: Subjective brain fog equated with objective cognitive impairment.
recommended_next_queries: Medication and substance effects on fatigue, mood, cognition, HRV, sleep, and GI symptoms.
summary.overall_assessment: Gut-brain evidence is promising and clinically relevant but heavily confounded. For BodyState Mapper, prioritize GI symptom burden and directly measured inflammatory/metabolomic markers; treat dysbiosis and intervention effects cautiously.

works_cited.title: Gut-Brain Axis, Microbiome, GI Symptoms, and Neurobehavioral Outcomes: Human Evidence Review
